The **politics of Canada** functions within a framework of [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") and a [federal system](/wiki/Federation "Federation") of [parliamentary government](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada") with strong democratic traditions.[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") is a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") where the [monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada") is the ceremonial [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"). In practice, executive authority is entrusted to the [Cabinet](/wiki/Canadian_Cabinet "Canadian Cabinet"), a committee of [ministers of the Crown](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Crown "Minister of the Crown") chaired by the [prime minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") that act as the executive committee of the [King's Privy Council for Canada](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "King's Privy Council for Canada") and are responsible to the democratically elected [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada").[[2]](#cite_note-Tidridge2011-2)

Canada is described as a "[full democracy](/wiki/Democracy_Index#Definitions "Democracy Index")",[[3]](#cite_note-3) with a tradition of [secular liberalism](/wiki/Secular_liberalism "Secular liberalism"),[[4]](#cite_note-WesthuesWharf2014-4) and an [egalitarian](/wiki/Egalitarian "Egalitarian"),[[5]](#cite_note-BickertonGagnon2009-5) [moderate](/wiki/Political_moderate "Political moderate") political ideology.[[6]](#cite_note-Johnson2016-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-BittnerKoop2013a-8) Extremism has never been prominent in Canadian politics.[[9]](#cite_note-a838-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Geddes_2022-12)[[13]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_2022-13)[[14]](#cite_note-m382-14) The traditional "brokerage" model of Canadian politics leaves little room for ideology, but on occasion ideologically-based parties have won representation or even dominance at the provincial level, such as in the cases of the [Co-operative Commonwealth Federation](/wiki/Co-operative_Commonwealth_Federation "Co-operative Commonwealth Federation"), the [Social Credit Party of Canada](/wiki/Social_Credit_Party_of_Canada "Social Credit Party of Canada") and the [Communist Party of Canada](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Canada "Communist Party of Canada").[[a]](#cite_note-politics-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) [Peace, order, and good government](/wiki/Peace,_order,_and_good_government "Peace, order, and good government"), alongside an [Implied Bill of Rights](/wiki/Implied_Bill_of_Rights "Implied Bill of Rights"), are founding principles of the Canadian government.[[16]](#cite_note-DixonScheurell2016-17)[[17]](#cite_note-Boughey2017-18) An emphasis on [multiculturalism](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") and [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice") has been a distinguishing element of Canada's political culture.[[18]](#cite_note-Fierlbeck2006-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) Canada has placed emphasis on [diversity, equity and inclusion](/wiki/Diversity,_equity_and_inclusion "Diversity, equity and inclusion") for all its people.[[21]](#cite_note-GuoWong2015-22)[[22]](#cite_note-McQuaig_2010_p._14-23)

The country has a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system") in which many of its legislative practices derive from the unwritten [conventions](/wiki/Convention_(norm) "Convention (norm)") of and precedents set by the [Westminster parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") of the United Kingdom. The [two dominant political parties](/wiki/Two-party_system "Two-party system") in Canada have historically been the [Liberal Party of Canada](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") and the current [Conservative Party of Canada](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada") (as well as its numerous [predecessors](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada#Predecessors "Conservative Party of Canada")).[[23]](#cite_note-Lansford2019-24) Parties like the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party_(Canada) "New Democratic Party (Canada)"), the [Quebec nationalist](/wiki/Quebec_nationalist "Quebec nationalist") [Bloc Québécois](/wiki/Bloc_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Bloc Québécois") and the [Green Party of Canada](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Canada "Green Party of Canada") have grown in prominence, exerting their own influence to the political process.[[23]](#cite_note-Lansford2019-24)

Canada has evolved variations: [party discipline](/wiki/Party_discipline "Party discipline") in Canada is stronger than in the United States and United Kingdom, and more parliamentary votes are considered [motions of confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence"), which tends to diminish the role of non-[Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)") members of parliament (MPs). Such members, in the government [caucus](/wiki/Caucus "Caucus"), and junior or lower-profile members of [opposition](/wiki/Parliamentary_opposition "Parliamentary opposition") caucuses, are known as [backbenchers](/wiki/Backbencher "Backbencher"). Backbenchers can, however, exert their influence by sitting in parliamentary committees, like the [Public Accounts Committee](/wiki/Canadian_House_of_Commons_Standing_Committee_on_Public_Accounts "Canadian House of Commons Standing Committee on Public Accounts") or the [National Defence Committee](/wiki/Canadian_House_of_Commons_Standing_Committee_on_Public_Safety_and_National_Security "Canadian House of Commons Standing Committee on Public Safety and National Security").

## Context

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Context")]

Canada's governmental structure was originally established by the [British Parliament](/wiki/British_Parliament "British Parliament") through the *British North America Act, 1867* (now the *[Constitution Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867")*),[[24]](#cite_note-CA67-25) but the federal model and division of powers were devised by Canadian politicians. Particularly after World War I, citizens of the self-governing [Dominions](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion"), such as Canada, began to develop a strong sense of identity, and, in the [Balfour Declaration of 1926](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration_of_1926 "Balfour Declaration of 1926"), the British government and the governments of the six Dominions jointly agreed that the Dominions had full autonomy within the British Commonwealth.

In 1931, after further consultations and agreements between the British government and the governments of the Dominions, the British Parliament passed the [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931"), giving legal recognition to the autonomy of Canada and other Dominions. However, Canadian politicians were unable to obtain consensus on a process for amending the constitution, which was therefore not affected by the Statute of Westminster, meaning amendments to Canada's constitution continued to require the approval of the British parliament until that date. Similarly, the [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council") in Britain continued to make the final decision on criminal appeals until 1933 and on civil appeals until 1949.[[25]](#cite_note-26) It was not until 1982, with the [Patriation](/wiki/Patriation "Patriation") of the Constitution, that the role of the British Parliament was ended.

## Political culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political culture")]

Further information: [Liberalism in Canada](/wiki/Liberalism_in_Canada "Liberalism in Canada") and [Conservatism in Canada](/wiki/Conservatism_in_Canada "Conservatism in Canada")

Canada's [egalitarian](/wiki/Egalitarian "Egalitarian") approach to governance has emphasized [social welfare](/wiki/Social_programs_in_Canada "Social programs in Canada"), [economic freedom](/wiki/Economy_of_Canada "Economy of Canada"), and [multiculturalism](/wiki/Multiculturalism_in_Canada "Multiculturalism in Canada"), which is based on selective [economic migrants](/wiki/Economic_migrant "Economic migrant"), [social integration](/wiki/Social_integration "Social integration"), and [suppression](/wiki/Dissent "Dissent") of [far-right politics](/wiki/Far-right_politics "Far-right politics"), that has wide public and political support.[[26]](#cite_note-Ambrosea-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28) Its broad range of constituent nationalities and policies that promote a "[just society](/wiki/Just_society "Just society")" are constitutionally protected.[[28]](#cite_note-Rand_Dyck_2011_88-29)[[29]](#cite_note-Newman2012-30) Individual rights, equality and inclusiveness ([social equality](/wiki/Social_equality "Social equality")) have risen to the forefront of political and legal importance for most [Canadians](/wiki/Canadians "Canadians"), as demonstrated through support for the *[Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms](/wiki/Canadian_Charter_of_Rights_and_Freedoms "Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms")*, a relatively free economy, and social liberal attitudes toward [women's rights](/wiki/Feminism_in_Canada "Feminism in Canada") (like [abortion](/wiki/Abortion_in_Canada "Abortion in Canada")), [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce"), [homosexuality](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality"), [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage"), [birth control](/wiki/Birth_control "Birth control"), [euthanasia](/wiki/Euthanasia_in_Canada "Euthanasia in Canada") or [cannabis use](/wiki/Cannabis_in_Canada "Cannabis in Canada").[[30]](#cite_note-HollifieldMartin2014b-31)[[28]](#cite_note-Rand_Dyck_2011_88-29)[[29]](#cite_note-Newman2012-30)[[21]](#cite_note-GuoWong2015-22)[[31]](#cite_note-32) There is also a sense of collective responsibility in Canadian political culture, as is demonstrated in general support for [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care"), multiculturalism, [evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution"), [gun control](/wiki/Gun_control "Gun control"), [foreign aid](/wiki/Foreign_aid "Foreign aid"), and [other social programs](/wiki/Social_programs_in_Canada "Social programs in Canada").[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-polls-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36)

| External videos | |
| --- | --- |
| [video icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_kaboodle.svg) ["Is the Left/Right political spectrum outdated?"](https://globalnews.ca/video/6336235/is-the-left-right-political-spectrum-outdated) – [Global News](/wiki/Global_News "Global News"), 2019 (7:23 mins). | |

At the federal level, Canada has been dominated by two relatively [centrist](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") parties practising "brokerage politics",[[a]](#cite_note-politics-15)[[38]](#cite_note-Brooks2004-39)[[39]](#cite_note-Smith2014-40)[[40]](#cite_note-Johnson2016c-41) the [centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left_politics "Centre-left politics") leaning [Liberal Party of Canada](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") and the [centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right_politics "Centre-right politics") leaning [Conservative Party of Canada](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada") (or its [predecessors](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada#Predecessors "Conservative Party of Canada")).[[41]](#cite_note-BaumerGold2015-42) "The traditional *brokerage* model of Canadian politics leaves little
room for ideology"[[42]](#cite_note-43) as the Canadian [catch-all party](/wiki/Big_tent "Big tent") system requires support from a broad spectrum of voters.[[36]](#cite_note-MarlandGiasson2012-37)[[39]](#cite_note-Smith2014-40)[[40]](#cite_note-Johnson2016c-41)[[38]](#cite_note-Brooks2004-39) The historically predominant Liberals position themselves at the centre of the political scale,[[43]](#cite_note-Gill_2021_p._485-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-Johnston2017-46) with the Conservatives sitting on the right and the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party "New Democratic Party") occupying the [left](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics").[[46]](#cite_note-BittnerKoop2013b-47)[[47]](#cite_note-EvansGraaf2013-48)[[48]](#cite_note-Carlisle2005b-49) Five parties had representatives elected to the federal parliament in the [2025 election](/wiki/2025_Canadian_federal_election "2025 Canadian federal election"): the Liberal Party who currently form the government, the Conservative Party who are the [Official Opposition](/wiki/Official_Opposition_(Canada) "Official Opposition (Canada)"), the New Democratic Party, the [Bloc Québécois](/wiki/Bloc_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Bloc Québécois"), and the [Green Party of Canada](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Canada "Green Party of Canada").

Polls have suggested that Canadians generally do not have a solid understanding of [civics](/wiki/Civics "Civics").[[49]](#cite_note-50) This has been theorized to be a result of less attention being given to the subject in provincial education curricula, beginning in the 1960s.[[50]](#cite_note-51) By 2008, a poll showed only 24 per cent of respondents could name the monarch as [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state").[[51]](#cite_note-52) Likewise, Senator [Lowell Murray](/wiki/Lowell_Murray "Lowell Murray") wrote five years earlier that "the Crown has become irrelevant to most Canadians' understanding of our system of Government."[[52]](#cite_note-53) As John Robson of the *[National Post](/wiki/National_Post "National Post")* opined in 2015: "Intellectually, voters and commentators succumb to the mistaken notion that we elect 'governments' of prime ministers and cabinets with untrammelled authority, that indeed ideal 'democracy' consists precisely in this kind of [plebiscitary](/wiki/Plebiscite "Plebiscite") [autocracy](/wiki/Autocracy "Autocracy")."[[53]](#cite_note-54) Instead under our election system, seats are allocated based on separate district contests; the prime minister is named by a party that holds majority of seats in the House of Commons or survives in that position by being supported by a working majority of multiple parties in the House of Commons; and governments remain in power only as long as they have support of a majority in the House, whether that group was elected by a majority of voters or not.

## Governmental organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Governmental organization")]

Main article: [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada")

Type of government
:   [Westminster style](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") [federal](/wiki/Federation "Federation") [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") within a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy").

Administrative divisions
:   [Ten provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Canada "Provinces of Canada") and [three territories](/wiki/Territories_of_Canada "Territories of Canada")\*: [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"), [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador"), [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories")\*, [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut")\*, [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island"), [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"), [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon")\*.

Constitution
:   [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), based on unwritten [conventions](/wiki/Constitutional_convention_(political_custom) "Constitutional convention (political custom)") and written [legislation](/wiki/Constitution_of_Canada "Constitution of Canada").

Legal system
:   [English](/wiki/English_law "English law") [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") for all matters within federal jurisdiction and in all provinces and territories except Quebec, which is based on the [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)"), based on the Custom of Paris in pre-revolutionary France as set out in the [Civil Code of Quebec](/wiki/Civil_Code_of_Quebec "Civil Code of Quebec"); accepts compulsory [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") jurisdiction, with reservations.

Further information: [Law of Canada](/wiki/Law_of_Canada "Law of Canada")

Suffrage
:   Citizens aged 18 years or older. Only two adult citizens in Canada cannot vote: the [Chief Electoral Officer](/wiki/Chief_Electoral_Officer_(Canada) "Chief Electoral Officer (Canada)"), and the Deputy Chief Electoral Officer. The [Governor General](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") is eligible to vote, but abstains due to [constitutional convention](/wiki/Constitutional_convention_(political_custom) "Constitutional convention (political custom)").

### Monarchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Monarchy")]

[Head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state")
:   [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), [King of Canada](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada") (since 8 September 2022).

[Viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy")
:   [Mary Simon](/wiki/Mary_Simon "Mary Simon"), [Governor General of Canada](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") (since July 26, 2021).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/King_Charles_III_%28July_2023%29.jpg/250px-King_Charles_III_%28July_2023%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Charles_III_(July_2023).jpg)

[Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), King of Canada, the country's [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Mary_Simon%2C_Governor_General_of_Canada.jpg/250px-Mary_Simon%2C_Governor_General_of_Canada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mary_Simon,_Governor_General_of_Canada.jpg)

[Mary Simon](/wiki/Mary_Simon "Mary Simon"), Governor General of Canada, the monarch's representative

> The function of constitutional monarchy is to personify the democratic state, to sanction legitimate authority, to assure the legality of means, and guarantee the execution of the public will.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

[Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), Queen of Canada, [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"), 1964

Canada is a constitutional monarchy, wherein the role of the reigning [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereign "Sovereign") is both legal and practical, but not political.[[55]](#cite_note-Forsey-56) The [monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada") is formally vested with all powers of state,[[56]](#cite_note-57) which are in practice exercised only by the various institutions of government acting under the sovereign's authority.[[57]](#cite_note-Smith6-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-Murdoch-61) The executive is thus formally referred to as the *King-in-Council*, the legislature as the *King-in-Parliament*, and the courts as the *King-on-the-Bench*.[[61]](#cite_note-MacLeod17-62)

Though the person who is monarch of Canada (currently [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")) is also the monarch of [14 other countries](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), he nevertheless reigns separately as [King of Canada](/wiki/Style_of_the_Canadian_sovereign "Style of the Canadian sovereign"), an office that is "truly Canadian" and "totally independent from that of the [monarch of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") or the other Commonwealth realms."[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64) On the advice of the Canadian prime minister, the sovereign appoints a federal [viceregal representative](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy")—the [governor general](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") (currently [Mary Simon](/wiki/Mary_Simon "Mary Simon"))—who, since 1947, is permitted to exercise almost all of the monarch's [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"); though, there are some duties which must be specifically performed by the monarch themselves (such as assent of certain bills). In case of the governor general's absence or incapacitation, the [administrator of Canada](/wiki/Administrator_of_Canada "Administrator of Canada") performs the Crown's most basic functions.

[Royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") is required to enact laws. As part of the royal prerogative, the [royal sign-manual](/wiki/Royal_sign-manual "Royal sign-manual") gives authority to [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") and [orders-in-Council](/wiki/Order_in_Council "Order in Council"). The royal prerogative also includes summoning, [proroguing](/wiki/Prorogation_in_Canada "Prorogation in Canada"), and [dissolving Parliament](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament#Canada "Dissolution of parliament") in order to [call an election](/wiki/Dropping_the_writ "Dropping the writ") and extends to foreign affairs, which include the negotiation and ratification of treaties, alliances, international agreements, and [declarations of war](/wiki/Declaration_of_war_by_Canada "Declaration of war by Canada");[[64]](#cite_note-65) the accreditation of Canadian diplomats and receipt of foreign diplomats; and the [issuance of passports](/wiki/Canadian_passport "Canadian passport").[[65]](#cite_note-66)

Despite the wide theoretical extent of the sovereign's powers, in actual practice the sovereign exercises them only through or with the advice of the Prime Minister and [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada").[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68) Within the [constitutional conventions](/wiki/Constitutional_conventions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitutional conventions of the United Kingdom") of the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), the sovereign's freedom of action or personal choice in any area of governance is quite limited.[[68]](#cite_note-MacLeod16-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70)

### Executive power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Executive power")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Prime_Ministers_of_Canada_to_1963.jpg/250px-Prime_Ministers_of_Canada_to_1963.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Ministers_of_Canada_to_1963.jpg)

[Canada's Prime Ministers](/wiki/List_of_prime_ministers_of_Canada "List of prime ministers of Canada") from 1867 to 1963. The [Prime Minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") serves as the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government").

[Head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government")
:   [Mark Carney](/wiki/Mark_Carney "Mark Carney"), [Prime Minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") (since March 14, 2025).

Cabinet
:   [Ministers](/wiki/The_Ministry "The Ministry") (usually around thirty) chosen by the prime minister and appointed by the governor general to lead various ministries and agencies, generally with regional representation. Traditionally most, if not all, cabinet ministers will be members of the leader's own party in the House of Commons or Senate (see [Cabinet of Canada](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada")); however this is not legally or constitutionally mandated, and occasionally, the prime minister will appoint a cabinet minister from another party.

Elections
:   The monarchy is hereditary. The governor general is appointed by the monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister for a non-specific term, though it is traditionally approximately five years. Following legislative elections, the leader of the majority party in the House of Commons is usually designated by the governor general to become prime minister.

Further information: [Monarchy of Canada](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada"), [Monarchy in the Canadian provinces](/wiki/Monarchy_in_the_Canadian_provinces "Monarchy in the Canadian provinces"), [Lieutenant Governor (Canada)](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant Governor (Canada)"), [Premier (Canada)](/wiki/Premier_(Canada) "Premier (Canada)"), and [Elections in Canada](/wiki/Elections_in_Canada "Elections in Canada")

### Legislative power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Legislative power")]

The [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") [Parliament of Canada](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada") consists of three parts: the [monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada"), the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"), and the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada").

Currently, the Senate, which is frequently described as providing regional representation, has 105 members appointed by the Governor-General on the advice of the Prime Minister to serve until age 75. It was created with equal representation from the three regions of Ontario, Quebec, and the Maritimes (originally New Brunswick and Nova Scotia, expanded in 1873 to include Prince Edward Island). In 1915, a new Western division was created, with six senators from each of the four western provinces, so that each of the four regions had 24 seats in the Senate. When Newfoundland and Labrador joined Confederation in 1949, it was not included in an existing region and was assigned six seats. Each of the three territories has one seat. It is not based on representation-by-population. The normal number of senators can be exceeded by the Governor General at the direction of the monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister, as long as the additional senators are distributed equally with regard to region (up to a total of eight additional senators). This power of additional appointment has only been used once, when Prime Minister [Brian Mulroney](/wiki/Brian_Mulroney "Brian Mulroney") petitioned [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") to direct that eight seats be added to the Senate so as to ensure the passage of the [Goods and Services Tax](/wiki/Goods_and_Services_Tax_(Canada) "Goods and Services Tax (Canada)") legislation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Colline_du_Parlement%2C_%C3%89difice_du_Centre_04.jpg/250px-Colline_du_Parlement%2C_%C3%89difice_du_Centre_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colline_du_Parlement,_%C3%89difice_du_Centre_04.jpg)

A democratically elected body, the [House of Commons of Canada](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") is one of three components of the [Parliament of Canada](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada").

The House of Commons currently has 343 members elected in [single-member districts](/wiki/Single-member_district "Single-member district") in a [plurality voting system](/wiki/Plurality_voting "Plurality voting") (first past the post), meaning that members must attain only a [plurality](/wiki/Plurality_(voting) "Plurality (voting)") (the most votes of any candidate) rather than a [majority](/wiki/Majority "Majority"). The [electoral districts](/wiki/Electoral_district_(Canada) "Electoral district (Canada)") are also known as [ridings](/wiki/Riding_(division) "Riding (division)").

Mandates cannot exceed five years; an election must occur by the end of this time. This fixed mandate has been exceeded only once, when Prime Minister [Robert Borden](/wiki/Robert_Borden "Robert Borden") perceived the need to do so during World War I. A [constitutional amendment](/wiki/British_North_America_Acts#British_North_America_Act,_1916 "British North America Acts") was passed, extending the life of the Parliament by one year, by the unanimous consent of the House of Commons. The size of the House and [apportionment](/wiki/Apportionment_(politics) "Apportionment (politics)") of seats to each province is revised after every decennial census, conducted every ten years, and is based on population changes and approximately on representation-by-population.

### Elections and government formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Elections and government formation")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Politics_of_Canada "Special:EditPage/Politics of Canada") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(October 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[Canadians](/wiki/Canadians "Canadians") vote for the election of their local [member of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Canada) "Member of Parliament (Canada)") (MP) only. A vote is cast directly for a candidate. Most candidates affiliate themselves to a [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party"), but candidates can run as [independents](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician"). The candidate in each riding who receives a plurality of votes (single member plurality or so-called [first-past-the-post system](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting")) is elected, whether or not they receive a majority of district votes.[[70]](#cite_note-71) An MP need not be a member of any political party. MPs may sit as [independents](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician").

The *[Canada Elections Act](/wiki/Canada_Elections_Act "Canada Elections Act")* defines a political party as "an organization one of whose fundamental purposes is to participate in public affairs by endorsing one or more of its members as candidates and supporting their election." Forming and registering a federal political party are two different things. There is no legislation regulating the formation of federal political parties. [Elections Canada](/wiki/Elections_Canada "Elections Canada") cannot dictate how a federal political party should be formed or how its legal, internal and financial structures should operate.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

Most parties elect their leaders in [instant-runoff elections](/wiki/Instant-runoff_voting "Instant-runoff voting") to ensure that the winner receives more than 50% of the votes. This happens at [leadership conventions](/wiki/Leadership_convention "Leadership convention"). Normally the party leader stands as a candidate to be an MP during an election. Canada's parliamentary system empowers political parties and their party leaders. Where one party gets a majority of the seats in the House of Commons, that party is said to have a "majority government" and by itself has right to form the cabinet and pass laws. Through party discipline, the party leader, who if elected sits in only one riding, exercises control over the cabinet and the parliament.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

Historically, the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") and senators are selected by the [governor general](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") as a representative of the King, though in modern practice the monarch's duties are ceremonial. Consequently, the prime minister, while technically selected by the Governor General, is for all practical purposes selected by the party with the majority of seats (or the party that can get support from a majority of MPs). That is, the party that gets the most seats normally forms the government, with that party's leader becoming prime minister. The prime minister is not directly elected by the general population, although the prime minister is almost always elected as an MP within a single riding.

Often elections give one party a majority of the seats in the House of Commons, even if the party did not receive a majority of the vote. However, as there are usually three or more political parties represented in the House of Commons, often no party takes a majority of the seats. A [minority government](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government") occurs when no party in the House of Commons holds more seats than the opposition parties combined. However, for the new government to survive and to pass laws, the leader chosen must have the support of the majority of the House, meaning they need the support of the elected members of at least one other party. This can be done on a case-by-case basis, through a coalition government (which has only occurred once at the federal level, the [Unionist government](/wiki/10th_Canadian_Ministry "10th Canadian Ministry") formed during the First World War) or through a [confidence-and-supply agreement](/wiki/Confidence-and-supply_agreement "Confidence-and-supply agreement") (such as the one the Liberals and the NDP signed in 2022).

If a party in power does not get support of a majority of MPs in a vote in the House of Commons, the defeated ministry may choose to stay in office until defeated on a vote of confidence in the House, or it may resign. If it resigns, the governor general may call an election immediately or may ask the leader of an opposition party to form a government.[[73]](#cite_note-w170-74) And again that new governing party has to aggregate support of a majority of MPs in the House of Commons, if it is to hold its position as governing party.

As a federation, the existence and powers of the federal government and the ten [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") are guaranteed by the Constitution. The *Constitution Act, 1867* sets out the basic constitutional structure of the federal government and the provinces.[[24]](#cite_note-CA67-25) The powers of the federal Parliament and the provinces can only be changed by constitutional amendments passed by the federal and provincial governments.[[74]](#cite_note-75) The Sovereign is the formal head of state of the federal government and each of the ten provinces, but has no political role. The governments are led by the representatives of the people: elected by all adult Canadians at the federal level, and by the citizens of each province at the provincial level.

Federal-provincial (or intergovernmental, formerly Dominion-provincial) relations is a regular issue in Canadian politics: Quebec wishes to preserve and strengthen its distinctive nature, western provinces desire more control over their abundant natural resources, especially energy reserves; industrialized Central Canada is concerned with its manufacturing base, and the Atlantic provinces strive to escape from being less affluent than the rest of the country.[[75]](#cite_note-:0-76)

In order to ensure that social programs such as health care and education are funded consistently throughout Canada, the "have-not" (poorer) provinces receive a proportionately greater share of federal "[transfer (equalization) payments](/wiki/Equalization_payments "Equalization payments")" than the richer, or "have", provinces do; this has been somewhat controversial. The richer provinces often favour freezing transfer payments, or rebalancing the system in their favour, based on the claim that they already pay more in taxes than they receive in federal government services, and the poorer provinces often favour an increase on the basis that the amount of money they receive is not sufficient for their existing needs.[[75]](#cite_note-:0-76)

Particularly in the past decade, critics have argued that the federal government's exercise of its unlimited constitutional spending power has contributed to strained federal-provincial relations.[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80) This power allows the federal government to influence provincial policies, by offering funding in areas that the federal government cannot itself regulate. The federal spending power is not expressly set out in the *[Constitution Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867")*; however, in the words of the [Court of Appeal for Ontario](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_for_Ontario "Court of Appeal for Ontario") the power "can be inferred" from s. 91(1A), "the public debt and property".[[80]](#cite_note-81)

A prime example of an exercise of the spending power is the *[Canada Health Act](/wiki/Canada_Health_Act "Canada Health Act")*, which is a conditional grant of money to the provinces. Regulation of health services is, under the Constitution, a provincial responsibility. However, by making the funding available to the provinces under the *Canada Health Act* contingent upon delivery of services according to federal standards, the federal government has the ability to influence health care delivery.

### Quebec and Canadian politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Quebec and Canadian politics")]

Except for three short-lived transitional or minority governments, prime ministers from [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") led Canada continuously from 1968 to early 2006. People from Quebec led both [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") and [Progressive Conservative](/wiki/Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Progressive Conservative Party of Canada") governments in this period.

Monarchs, governors general, and prime ministers are now expected to be at least functional, if not fluent, in both [English](/wiki/Canadian_English "Canadian English") and [French](/wiki/French_language_in_Canada "French language in Canada"). In selecting leaders, political parties give preference to candidates who are fluently bilingual.

By law, three of the nine positions on the [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada") must be held by judges from Quebec. This representation makes sure that at least three judges have sufficient experience with the [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") system to treat cases involving Quebec laws.[[81]](#cite_note-2014_SCC_21-82)

## National unity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: National unity")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(October 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Canada has a long and storied history of secessionist movements (see [Secessionist movements of Canada](/wiki/Secessionist_movements_of_Canada "Secessionist movements of Canada")). National unity has been a major issue in Canada since the forced union of [Upper](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada") and [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada") in 1840.

The predominant and lingering issue concerning Canadian national unity has been the ongoing conflict between the French-speaking majority in Quebec and the English-speaking majority in the rest of Canada. Quebec's continued demands for recognition of its "[distinct society](/wiki/Distinct_society "Distinct society")" through special political status has led to attempts for constitutional reform, most notably with the failed attempts to amend the constitution through the [Meech Lake Accord](/wiki/Meech_Lake_Accord "Meech Lake Accord") and the [Charlottetown Accord](/wiki/Charlottetown_Accord "Charlottetown Accord") (the latter of which was rejected through a national [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum")).

Since the [Quiet Revolution](/wiki/Quiet_Revolution "Quiet Revolution"), sovereigntist sentiments in Quebec have been variably stoked by the [patriation of the Canadian constitution in 1982](/wiki/Canada_Act_1982 "Canada Act 1982") (without Quebec's consent) and by the failed attempts at constitutional reform. Two provincial referendums, in [1980](/wiki/1980_Quebec_referendum "1980 Quebec referendum") and [1995](/wiki/1995_Quebec_referendum "1995 Quebec referendum"), rejected proposals for sovereignty with majorities of 60% and 50.6% respectively. Given the narrow federalist victory in 1995, a reference was made by the [Chrétien](/wiki/Jean_Chr%C3%A9tien "Jean Chrétien") government to the [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada") in 1998 regarding the [legality of unilateral provincial secession](/wiki/Re_Secession_of_Quebec "Re Secession of Quebec"). The court decided that a unilateral declaration of secession would be unconstitutional. This resulted in the passage of the *[Clarity Act](/wiki/Clarity_Act "Clarity Act")* in 2000.

The [Bloc Québécois](/wiki/Bloc_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Bloc Québécois"), a sovereigntist party which runs candidates exclusively in [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), was started by a group of MPs who left the Progressive Conservative (PC) party (along with several disaffected Liberal MPs), and first put forward candidates in the 1993 federal election. With the collapse of the PCs in that election, the Bloc and Liberals were seen as the only two viable parties in Quebec. Thus, prior to the 2006 election, any gain by one party came at the expense of the other, regardless of whether national unity was really at issue. The Bloc, then, benefited (with a significant increase in seat total) from the impressions of corruption that surrounded the Liberal Party in the lead-up to the 2004 election. However, the newly unified Conservative party re-emerged as a viable party in Quebec by winning 10 seats in the 2006 election. In the 2011 election, the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party_(Canada) "New Democratic Party (Canada)") succeeded in winning 59 of Quebec's 75 seats, successfully reducing the number of seats of every other party substantially. The NDP surge nearly destroyed the Bloc, reducing them to 4 seats, far below the minimum requirement of 12 seats for [official party status](/wiki/Official_party_status "Official party status").

[Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador") is also a problem regarding national unity. As the [Dominion of Newfoundland](/wiki/Dominion_of_Newfoundland "Dominion of Newfoundland") was a self-governing country equal to Canada until 1949, there are large, though uncoordinated, feelings of Newfoundland nationalism and [anti-Canadian sentiment](/wiki/Anti-Canadian_sentiment "Anti-Canadian sentiment") among much of the population. This is due in part to the perception of chronic [federal mismanagement of the fisheries](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_Atlantic_northwest_cod_fishery "Collapse of the Atlantic northwest cod fishery"), [forced resettlement](/wiki/Resettlement_(Newfoundland) "Resettlement (Newfoundland)") away from isolated settlements in the 1960s, the government of Quebec still drawing inaccurate political maps whereby [they take parts of Labrador](/wiki/Labrador#Boundary_dispute "Labrador"), and to the perception that mainland Canadians look down upon Newfoundlanders. In 2004, the [Newfoundland and Labrador First Party](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador_First_Party "Newfoundland and Labrador First Party") contested provincial elections and in 2008 in federal ridings within the province. In 2004, then-premier [Danny Williams](/wiki/Danny_Williams_(Canadian_politician) "Danny Williams (Canadian politician)") ordered all federal flags removed from government buildings as a result of lost offshore revenues to equalization clawbacks.[[82]](#cite_note-cbc.ca-83) On December 23, 2004, premier Williams made this statement to reporters in [St. John's](/wiki/St._John%27s,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador"),

> They basically slighted us, they are not treating us as a proper partner in Confederation. It's intolerable and it's insufferable and these flags will be taken down indefinitely. It's also quite apparent to me that we were dragged to Manitoba in order to punish us, quite frankly, to try to embarrass us, to bring us out there to get no deal and send us back with our tail between our legs.

— Premier [Danny Williams](/wiki/Danny_Williams_(Canadian_politician) "Danny Williams (Canadian politician)")[[82]](#cite_note-cbc.ca-83)

[Western alienation](/wiki/Western_alienation "Western alienation") is another national-unity-related concept that enters into Canadian politics. Residents of the four western provinces, particularly Alberta, have often been unhappy with a lack of influence and a perceived lack of understanding when residents of Central Canada consider "national" issues. While this is seen to play itself out through many avenues (media, commerce, and so on.), in politics, it has given rise to a number of political parties whose base constituency is in western Canada. These include the [United Farmers of Alberta](/wiki/United_Farmers_of_Alberta "United Farmers of Alberta"), who first won federal seats in 1917, the [Progressives](/wiki/Progressive_Party_of_Canada "Progressive Party of Canada") (1921), the [Social Credit Party](/wiki/Social_Credit_Party_of_Canada "Social Credit Party of Canada") (1935), the [Co-operative Commonwealth Federation](/wiki/Co-operative_Commonwealth_Federation "Co-operative Commonwealth Federation") (1935), the [Reconstruction Party](/wiki/Reconstruction_Party "Reconstruction Party") (1935), [New Democracy](/wiki/New_Democracy_(Canada) "New Democracy (Canada)") (1940) and most recently the [Reform Party](/wiki/Reform_Party_of_Canada "Reform Party of Canada") (1989).

The Reform Party's slogan "The West Wants In" was echoed by commentators when, after a successful merger with the PCs, the successor party to both parties, the Conservative Party won the 2006 election. Led by Stephen Harper, who is an MP from Alberta, the electoral victory was said to have made "The West IS In" a reality. However, regardless of specific electoral successes or failures, the concept of western alienation continues to be important in Canadian politics, particularly on a provincial level, where opposing the federal government is a common tactic for provincial politicians. For example, in 2001, a group of prominent Albertans produced the [Alberta Agenda](/wiki/Alberta_Agenda "Alberta Agenda"), urging Alberta to take steps to make full use of its constitutional powers, much as Quebec has done.

## Political conditions

Canada is considered by most sources to be a very stable democracy. In 2006, *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* ranked Canada the third-most democratic nation in its [Democracy Index](/wiki/Democracy_Index "Democracy Index"), ahead of all other nations in the Americas and ahead of every nation more populous than itself. According to the [V-Dem Democracy indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_indices "V-Dem Democracy indices"), in 2023 Canada was the 19th most electoral democratic country in the world.[[83]](#cite_note-vdem_dataset-84)[[84]](#cite_note-85)

More recently, with the existence of strong third parties and first-past-the-post elections amongst other factors, Canada on a federal and provincial level has experienced huge swings in seat shares, where third parties (e.g. NDP, Reform) end up (usually briefly) replacing the Liberals, the Progressive Conservatives or the Conservatives as the main opposition or even the government and leaving them as a rump. Such examples federally include the [1993 federal election](/wiki/1993_Canadian_federal_election "1993 Canadian federal election") with the collapse of the Progressive Conservatives, and the [2011 election](/wiki/2011_Canadian_federal_election "2011 Canadian federal election") leaving the Liberal Party a (temporary) rump along with Bloc Québécois. Other examples include the changes of fortune for the Alberta NDP during the province's 2015 and 2019 elections, and possibly the 2018 Quebec elections with the rise of [Coalition Avenir Québec](/wiki/Coalition_Avenir_Qu%C3%A9bec "Coalition Avenir Québec") taking government from the Liberals and [Parti Québécois](/wiki/Parti_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Parti Québécois").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/GoverningPoliticalPartyByProvince_v2.png/250px-GoverningPoliticalPartyByProvince_v2.png)](/wiki/File:GoverningPoliticalPartyByProvince_v2.png)

The governing political party(s) in each Canadian province. Multicoloured provinces are governed by a coalition or minority government consisting of more than one party.

On a provincial level, in the legislatures of western provinces the NDP often is the left-leaning main party instead of that province's Liberal Party branch, the latter generally being a rump or smaller than the NDP. The other main party (right of the NDP) is either the Progressive Conservatives or their successor, or the Saskatchewan Party in Saskatchewan.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

### Party systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Party systems")]

Main article: [Party system § Canada](/wiki/Party_system#Canada "Party system")

According to recent scholars, there have been four party systems in [Canada] at the federal level since Confederation, each with its own distinctive pattern of social support, [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage") relationships, leadership styles, and electoral strategies.[[86]](#cite_note-87) Political scientists disagree on the names and precise boundaries of the eras, however. Steve Patten identifies four party systems in Canada's political history[[87]](#cite_note-88)

Clarkson (2005) shows how the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") has dominated all the party systems, using different approaches. It began with a "clientelistic approach" under [Laurier](/wiki/Sir_Wilfrid_Laurier "Sir Wilfrid Laurier"), which evolved into a "brokerage" system of the 1920s, 1930s and 1940s under [Mackenzie King](/wiki/William_Lyon_Mackenzie_King "William Lyon Mackenzie King"). The 1950s saw the emergence of a "pan-Canadian system", which lasted until the 1990s. The 1993 election — categorized by Clarkson as an electoral "earthquake" which "fragmented" the party system, saw the emergence of regional politics within a four party-system, whereby various groups championed regional issues and concerns. Clarkson concludes that the inherent bias built into the first-past-the-post system, has chiefly benefited the Liberals.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

### Party funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Party funding")]

The rules governing the funding of parties are designed to ensure reliance on personal contributions. Personal donations to federal parties and campaigns benefit from tax credits, although the amount of tax relief depends on the amount given. Also only people paying income taxes receive any benefit from this.

The rules are based on the belief that union or business funding should not be allowed to have as much impact on federal election funding as these are not contributions from citizens and are not evenly spread out between parties. The new rules stated that a party had to receive 2% of the vote nationwide in order to receive the general federal funding for parties. Each vote garnered a certain dollar amount for a party (approximately $1.75) in future funding. For the initial disbursement, approximations were made based on previous elections. The NDP received more votes than expected (its national share of the vote went up) while the new Conservative Party of Canada received fewer votes than had been estimated and was asked to refund the difference. [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") was the first province to implement a similar system of funding many years before the changes to funding of federal parties.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

[Federal funds](/wiki/Federal_political_financing_in_Canada "Federal political financing in Canada") are disbursed quarterly to parties, beginning at the start of 2005. For the moment, this disbursement delay leaves the NDP and the Green Party in a better position to fight an election, since they rely more on individual contributors than federal funds. The Green Party now receives federal funds, since it for the first time received a sufficient share of the vote in the 2004 election.[[90]](#cite_note-debates-91)

In 2007, news emerged of a funding loophole that "could cumulatively exceed the legal limit by more than $60,000", through anonymous recurrent donations of $200 to every riding of a party from corporations or unions. At the time, for each individual, the legal annual donation limit was $1,100 for each party, $1,100 combined total for each party's associations, and in an election year, an additional $1,100 combined total for each party's candidates. All three limits increase on 1 April every year based on the inflation rate.[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94)

Two of the biggest federal political parties in Canada experienced a drop in donations in 2020, in light of the COVID-19 pandemic impact on the global economy.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

### Leaders' debates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Leaders' debates")]

Main article: [Canadian leaders debates](/wiki/Canadian_leaders_debates "Canadian leaders debates")

Leaders' debates in Canada consist of two debates, one English and one French,[[95]](#cite_note-TarasWaddell2012-96) both produced by a consortium of Canada's five major television broadcasters ([CBC](/wiki/CBC_Television "CBC Television")/[SRC](/wiki/Ici_Radio-Canada_T%C3%A9l%C3%A9 "Ici Radio-Canada Télé"), [CTV](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network"), [Global](/wiki/Global_Television_Network "Global Television Network") and [TVA](/wiki/TVA_(Canada) "TVA (Canada)")) and usually consist of the leaders of all parties with representation in the House of Commons.

These debates air on the networks of the producing consortium as well as the public affairs and parliamentary channel [CPAC](/wiki/CPAC_(TV_channel) "CPAC (TV channel)") and the American public affairs network [C-SPAN](/wiki/C-SPAN "C-SPAN").

## Government departments and structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Canada&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Government departments and structure")]

The Canadian government operates the public service using departments, smaller agencies (for example, commissions, tribunals, and boards), and crown corporations. There are two types of departments: central agencies such as Finance, Privy Council Office, and Treasury Board Secretariat have an organizing and oversight role for the entire public service; line departments are departments that perform tasks in a specific area or field, such as the departments of Agriculture, Environment, or Defence.

* Significant departments include [Finance](/wiki/Department_of_Finance_(Canada) "Department of Finance (Canada)"), [Revenue](/wiki/Canada_Revenue_Agency "Canada Revenue Agency"), [Human Resources and Skills Development](/wiki/Human_Resources_and_Skills_Development_Canada "Human Resources and Skills Development Canada"), [National Defence](/wiki/Department_of_National_Defence_(Canada) "Department of National Defence (Canada)"), [Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness](/wiki/Public_Safety_and_Emergency_Preparedness_Canada "Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness Canada"), and [Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Department_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Canada) "Department of Foreign Affairs (Canada)")/[International Trade](/wiki/Department_of_International_Trade_(Canada) "Department of International Trade (Canada)").

Further information: [Structure of the Canadian federal government](/wiki/Structure_of_the_Canadian_federal_government "Structure of the Canadian federal government")

Scholar Peter Aucoin, writing about the Canadian Westminster system, raised concerns in the early 2000s about the centralization of power; an increased number, role and influence of partisan-political staff; personal-politicization of appointments to the senior public service; and the assumption that the public service is promiscuously partisan for the government of the day.[[96]](#cite_note-97)
